author: @saifcathum3423
text: I started building E-Bikes last year, and I can tell you that doesn't look like 250w or even 500w motor. I would say that motor is closer to 1000W - 2000W, unless the motor is mostly hollow inside. The watt rating is less important than the winding configuration and the total mass of the motor (more mass can handle more heat). Crack it open and take a look inside. While you have it open add some Starorade from GRIN Tech (air gap bridging ferrofluid for cooling). Then that motor should easily handle 72V at 60A without over heating. You may need upgrade the phase wires to 10 Gauge to handle the current.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
the brake on the front won't be problematic will it?  I remember on my old small motorbike, one had to be very careful with the front brake or the back end would either try to flip, or swing out to the side- both of which were alarming and caused crashes early on.   Just something to ponder maybe.

Also for some reason I had assumed you'd put the drive wheel on the front.  Don't know Why I thought that, but I did.

replies.author: @another3997
replies.text: It's no different to applying braking on a bicycle, or indeed any vehicle. You modulate the braking. On a bike or scooter, you can lean your backside rearwards to counteract some of the weight shift and avoid any embarrassing ground to flesh interfacing. Around 75% of the braking ability is provided by the front wheel, and hard braking at the rear will just cause a skid.  Because static friction is less than rolling friction, a locked wheel takes longer to stop than one that is not quite skidding.
